MIT.name: MIT License
MIT.summary: Permissive license with no restrictions on use, distribution, or modification. Attribution required.

Apache-2.0.name: Apache License 2.0
Apache-2.0.summary: Permissive license with explicit patent grant. Attribution and license notice required.

BSD-2-Clause.name: BSD 2-Clause "Simplified" License
BSD-2-Clause.summary: Permissive license requiring attribution in source and binary forms.

BSD-3-Clause.name: BSD 3-Clause "New" or "Revised" License
BSD-3-Clause.summary: Permissive license. Adds clause prohibiting use of project name for endorsement.

ISC.name: ISC License
ISC.summary: Functionally equivalent to MIT. Simple permissive license.

Unlicense.name: The Unlicense
Unlicense.summary: Dedication to public domain. No restrictions whatsoever.

CC0-1.0.name: Creative Commons Zero v1.0 Universal
CC0-1.0.summary: Public domain dedication. No copyright reserved.

MPL-2.0.name: Mozilla Public License 2.0
MPL-2.0.summary: Weak copyleft at file level. Modified files must remain MPL. Can be combined with proprietary code.

LGPL-2.1.name: GNU Lesser General Public License v2.1
LGPL-2.1.summary: Weak copyleft. Can be linked from proprietary software. Modifications to the library itself must be shared.

LGPL-3.0.name: GNU Lesser General Public License v3.0
LGPL-3.0.summary: Same as LGPL-2.1 but based on GPL-3. Adds patent termination and anti-tivoization clauses.

GPL-2.0.name: GNU General Public License v2.0
GPL-2.0.summary: Strong copyleft. Any software that includes GPL-2.0 code must also be GPL-2.0. Source must be distributed.

GPL-3.0.name: GNU General Public License v3.0
GPL-3.0.summary: Strong copyleft with patent and anti-tivoization protections. Source distribution required.

AGPL-3.0.name: GNU Affero General Public License v3.0
AGPL-3.0.summary: Like GPL-3.0 but closes the SaaS loophole. Running modified AGPL software over a network requires sharing source.

SSPL-1.0.name: Server Side Public License v1
SSPL-1.0.summary: Created by MongoDB. Offering the software as a service requires open sourcing your entire service stack including management, monitoring, and backup infrastructure. Commercial SaaS use cases require careful review.
SSPL-1.0.realWorldImpact: MongoDB (2018), Elasticsearch (2021) switched to this. Cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Azure) forked or dropped support rather than comply.

BSL-1.1.name: Business Source License 1.1
BSL-1.1.summary: Source is available but commercial use is restricted until a specified date (usually 4 years after release), after which it converts to an open source license. Specific usage restrictions depend on the Additional Use Grant terms.
BSL-1.1.realWorldImpact: HashiCorp (Terraform, Vault, Consul) switched from MPL-2.0 to BSL-1.1 in 2023. Triggered OpenTofu fork by the community.

RSALv2.name: Redis Source Available License v2
RSALv2.summary: Source available license with restrictions on competitive use. Using the software to compete with Redis Ltd products or offering it as a managed database service requires closer review of license terms.
RSALv2.realWorldImpact: Redis switched from BSD-3-Clause to RSALv2 + SSPLv1 in 2024. AWS, GCP, Azure announced Valkey fork (now Linux Foundation project) in response.

CCL-1.0.name: CockroachDB Community License
CCL-1.0.summary: Source available license that allows most uses. Offering CockroachDB as a managed service or competing with Cockroach Labs' commercial offerings requires review of license terms.
CCL-1.0.realWorldImpact: CockroachDB uses dual licensing: Apache 2.0 for core + CCL for enterprise features. Similar to MongoDB's SSPL strategy.

CSL.name: CockroachDB Software License
CSL.summary: Proprietary source-available license. Commercial use and SaaS deployment may require a paid license from Cockroach Labs depending on your use case.
CSL.realWorldImpact: CockroachDB switched to CSL in November 2024, replacing CCL-1.0 with more restrictive terms.
